title: Brioche
subtitle: French enriched bread, rich with butter and eggs
excerpt: Classic French brioche: an enriched bread made with a high proportion of butter and eggs, giving a tender, golden crumb and a soft, peeling crust. Baked in a loaf tin. Makes one loaf.
sourceNotes: Brioche is documented in French household baking manuals from at least the seventeenth century. The Norman and Parisian forms are recorded in the Grande cuisine literature predating the twentieth century. The ratio of butter to flour (50–60% butter by baker's percentage) and the high egg content are consistent across French household and professional baking records. The loaf form recorded here follows the pain brioché documented in nineteenth-century French pâtisserie manuals, simpler than the Nanterre or the fluted tête shapes.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully cooled, sliced or whole. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The overnight cold proof (step 2) is the standard method. The cold temperature slows fermentation and makes the dough easier to shape.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 190°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A bread dough that contains fat, eggs, sugar, or dairy in addition to the basic flour, water, salt, and yeast. The added fat and eggs coat the gluten strands, producing a tenderer crumb than a lean dough. Enriched doughs ferment more slowly (fat inhibits yeast activity), take longer to develop full gluten structure, and benefit from a cold overnight retard to firm the butter and make shaping manageable.

content.text: that requires more time than effort. The key step is adding the butter in small pieces after the initial gluten has developed — dropping it in all at once produces a greasy dough that never properly comes together. A stand mixer handles this most easily; the dough can be developed by hand but takes 20–25 minutes of sustained kneading.
Develop the dough and add the butter

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{yeast-dried}} in a large bowl. Add the beaten {{eggs}} and {{whole-milk}} and mix to a shaggy dough. Knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. With the mixer running on medium speed (or kneading by hand), add the cubed softened {{unsalted-butter}} piece by piece over 10 minutes, waiting for each addition to be fully incorporated before adding the next. The dough will pass through a greasy stage before coming back together. Knead for a further 5 minutes until smooth, glossy, and slightly tacky.
Bulk ferment, retard, shape, proof, and bake

Cover the bowl and leave at room temperature for 2 hours until roughly doubled. Then knock back, shape into a ball, cover tightly, and refrigerate overnight or for up to 16 hours. The next day, remove from the fridge, divide into four equal pieces, and place in a greased and lined 900 g loaf tin. Leave at room temperature for 60–90 minutes, until the dough has risen to just above the rim of the tin. Brush the surface gently with beaten egg.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Bake for 28–32 minutes, until deep amber and the base sounds hollow when tapped. If the top darkens too quickly, lay a sheet of foil loosely over it after 20 minutes. Cool on a rack for at least 30 minutes before slicing.
